title: External Filter Tricks
subtitle: {key:!}{motion}cmd — let any Unix tool transform your text.
summary: Vim's filter command !{motion} sends the text covered by motion as stdin to a shell command, replacing it with stdout. Sort, dedup, format, encode, decode — any tool works.

blocks.text: Vim's filter operator (Normal-mode {key:!}) sends a chunk of the buffer through a shell command and replaces it with the result. Combined with Unix tools, you have unlimited transforms.

rows: Sort current line through `sort` (no-op for one line, but legal)
Sort from cursor to end of file

blocks.title: How Filter Mode Works
blocks.text:
From Normal mode, {key:!}{motion} prompts for a shell command on the command line. When you press {key:Enter}:

  1. The lines covered by `motion` are written to a temp file.
  2. The shell command is run with that file as stdin.
  3. The motion's lines are replaced in the buffer with the command's stdout.

{key:!}{key:!} (doubled bang) targets the current line. From Ex, `:[range]!cmd` does the same with an explicit range.

The filter is *line-oriented* even from Normal mode — {key:!}{key:i}{key:w} doesn't filter just a word; the entire line(s) containing the word are sent. (Use the substitute command for sub-line transforms.)
Make a habit of using filters for one-shot needs (sort, format, encode). It's faster than alt-tabbing to a shell and back.
